questions.prompt: What is the core idea of the Page Object Model?
options: Each page gets a class holding that page's locators and actions, so tests call methods instead of raw locators
Every test gets its own browser
All locators are stored in a single shared text file
Assertions are moved into the page class
questions.explanation: POM encapsulates a page's locators and actions in a dedicated class. Tests interact through action methods like login() and never reference raw locators directly.

questions.prompt: A field's id changes from user-name to username. With POM, how many places must you edit?
options: One — the locator in the page object class
Two — the test and the data provider
questions.explanation: Because the locator lives in exactly one place inside the page object, a markup change is a single-line fix there, and every test that uses the page keeps working.

questions.prompt: Where should an assertion live in a POM-based test?
options: Inside the page object's action method
In the test method; the page object only drives the UI
Assertions are not allowed when using POM
questions.explanation: A page object encapsulates locators and actions, not verdicts. The test decides pass or fail, keeping page objects reusable across tests that check different things.

questions.prompt: Besides easier maintenance, what is a benefit of POM for the test code itself?
options: Tests read as a clear flow of actions (loginPage.login(...)) instead of low-level locator calls
Tests automatically generate their own data
questions.explanation: By hiding locators behind named methods, POM makes tests describe what they do rather than how, so the test reads like a sequence of meaningful steps.
